primary_diagnosis.name: Invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma of the dorsum of the left hand, arising in a pre-existing actinic keratosis, with acute ulceration and haemorrhage

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly enlarging, firm, indurated, keratotic (cutaneous horn) nodule on chronically sun-exposed skin, arising in a pre-existing actinic keratosis, now ulcerated and bleeding — the classic macroscopic picture of an invasive SCC. Keratoacanthoma sits on the same SCC spectrum and is managed identically (urgent excision/biopsy), so it does not lower the urgency; the deep induration, tenderness and asymmetric ulceration favour invasive SCC over a symmetrical dome-shaped keratoacanthoma.

Another sun-related skin cancer that can ulcerate and bleed, but the rapid six-week growth, thick keratotic scale and deep firm induration favour SCC over the typically slow-growing, pearly, telangiectatic BCC.

The lesion began as an actinic keratosis, but the deep induration, rapid nodular growth and ulceration indicate it has evolved past a superficial precancerous lesion into invasive disease — it cannot be dismissed as a simple inflamed AK.
Any rapidly changing, ulcerating, bleeding lesion must not have melanoma missed; the keratotic scale and arising-in-AK history favour SCC, but only histology definitively excludes melanoma.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the AI Doctor should already frame this as a suspected high-risk skin malignancy needing urgent biopsy/excision, not as trivial trauma or a simple graze that happened to bleed.

differential.diagnosis: invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (high-risk features)
differential.evidence_basis: Rapid growth, size >2 cm, deep induration and fixation to underlying tissue, tenderness, ulceration with a necrotic base, arising in an AK on sun-damaged skin, plus a palpable regional (axillary) node — a constellation of high-risk SCC features. Deep induration, fixation and a palpable node argue for invasive SCC rather than a self-limited keratoacanthoma (same spectrum, identical urgent excision/biopsy management). The bleeding is mechanical from the fragile ulcerated surface, prolonged by apixaban.
Kept as the must-not-miss diagnosis until histology; definitive exclusion is by excision biopsy, which telehealth cannot perform.
No fever, no surrounding spreading erythema, warmth or purulence — an acute soft-tissue infection is not supported and antibiotics are not indicated for the bleed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen confirms a suspicious high-risk malignant lesion with controllable venous bleeding in a haemodynamically stable, anticoagulated patient, and no features of infection or shock.

differential.diagnosis: invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma with acute ulceration and haemorrhage
differential.evidence_basis: Clinically classic high-risk SCC; definitive diagnosis still requires histology. The immediate need is hemostasis; the definitive need is urgent fast-track excision/biopsy with regional nodal assessment. Keratoacanthoma, BCC and melanoma remain formally on the differential until the biopsy returns.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A telehealth-appropriate answer names a suspected high-risk skin cancer requiring urgent in-person hemostasis and a fast-track surgical/skin-cancer referral (with nodal assessment), without asserting a confirmed diagnosis and without over-escalating a stable, controllable venous bleed to a 000 ambulance.

red_flags.description: Rapidly enlarging, firm, indurated, keratotic, ulcerating nodule on chronically sun-exposed skin, arising in a pre-existing actinic keratosis — suspected high-risk invasive skin malignancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: A rapidly-growing, indurated, ulcerating keratotic nodule on sun-damaged skin is an invasive squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise and requires urgent tissue diagnosis and excision; delay allows local invasion and, in high-risk SCC, regional spread.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing it as trivial trauma or a simple bleeding graze leaves an untreated invasive skin cancer to grow, invade and potentially metastasise.

red_flags.description: Active, heavy spontaneous bleeding from the ulcerated lesion in a frail elderly patient on a direct oral anticoagulant (apixaban), not controlled by basic nursing pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Apixaban impairs coagulation so a fragile ulcerated tumour surface can bleed persistently; while currently a controllable venous ooze in a stable patient, it needs definitive hemostasis and can recur or worsen.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to achieve definitive hemostasis or to safety-net for uncontrolled bleeding risks clinically significant blood loss in a frail anticoagulated patient.

red_flags.description: Palpable regional (left axillary) lymph node with a deeply indurated lesion fixed/tethered to underlying tissue — possible nodal metastasis and deep invasion
red_flags.clinical_significance: A palpable regional node and a fixed, indurated primary raise the possibility of regional metastatic spread and deep invasion, mandating urgent fast-track surgical/oncology assessment and nodal evaluation rather than routine excision planning. These are clinician palpation findings, not obtainable over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating deep invasion and possible nodal disease can lead to inadequate treatment and delayed staging of an aggressive malignancy.

pathophysiology_summary: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma arises from malignant transformation of epidermal keratinocytes driven by cumulative ultraviolet DNA damage, typically progressing along a continuum from actinic keratosis (in-situ dysplasia) to invasive SCC — exactly the history here of a long-standing actinic keratosis that rapidly became a firm indurated nodule. Invasive SCC produces a keratinising, often hyperkeratotic tumour (the thick adherent scale / cutaneous horn) that outgrows its blood supply and ulcerates, exposing fragile neovascular tissue that bleeds readily with minor trauma; concurrent direct-oral-anticoagulant therapy (apixaban, a factor Xa inhibitor) prolongs that bleeding. Unlike basal cell carcinoma, high-risk SCC (rapid growth, size >2 cm, depth/induration, tenderness/perineural features, immunosuppression, high-risk sites) carries a meaningful risk of regional lymph-node metastasis, which is why a palpable draining node materially raises the stakes. Acute management is mechanical/chemical hemostasis; definitive management is urgent excision with histological margins and regional nodal assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the presentation as a simple bleeding graze or knocked scab and reassuring/discharging without arranging urgent biopsy/excision — missing an invasive high-risk skin cancer.

Reflexively sending a haemodynamically stable frail nursing-home resident by 000 ambulance to ED for a slow, controllable venous ooze — an unnecessary and itself-harmful over-escalation when firm sustained pressure plus urgent in-person attendance is appropriate.

Telling the patient to stop apixaban to control the bleed without weighing the atrial-fibrillation stroke risk — any change is a considered in-person clinician / proceduralist decision, not a telehealth reflex.

Trying to definitively 'manage it at home' or instructing home cautery/removal instead of arranging in-person hemostasis and a fast-track excision/biopsy pathway — telehealth cannot cauterise, dress, bandage or biopsy.

Mislabelling the ulcerated bleeding lesion as an infected wound and prescribing antibiotics, when there is no fever, spreading erythema, warmth or purulence — antibiotics do not treat a bleeding malignant ulcer.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for the depth of induration and fixation/tethering to underlying subcutaneous tissue
Palpation of regional (axillary and epitrochlear) lymph nodes
Histopathological diagnosis and margin assessment via biopsy or excision
Application of chemical cautery (silver nitrate), a professional alginate haemostatic dressing, or a firm crepe pressure bandage
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Direct the on-site registered nurse to apply firm, continuous, uninterrupted direct pressure over gauze for a full 20 minutes without lifting to peek, with the hand elevated above heart level; arrange URGENT same-day in-person attendance (facility GP / after-hours medical service / urgent care) for definitive hemostasis and a fast-track surgical/skin-cancer referral for excision, histology and regional nodal assessment; escalate to the emergency department if firm sustained pressure and elevation do not control the bleeding, or if signs of significant blood loss appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising a suspicious high-risk lesion (the morphology and history are classic). The real risks are UNDER-recognition (dismissing an invasive skin cancer as a trivial bleed) and OVER-triage (000 ambulance/ED as the baseline for a controllable venous ooze in a stable patient).
